title: Tired, strained eyes from screens
excerpt: Hours of screens leave your eyes dry, hot and aching. Ayurveda treats the eye as a seat of fire, and its old advice on rest, cooling and triphala still fits a workday glued to a glowing rectangle.
body_html:
that 5 pm screen ache

By late afternoon, many of us notice it: a dull ache behind the eyes, a gritty dryness, words on the screen starting to swim. We blink less than half as often when we stare at a phone or laptop, and the room is often air-conditioned and dry. The eyes, which were never built for hours of fixed near-focus in artificial light, quietly complain.

In Ayurveda the eye is called netra, and what we feel here is netra shrama, plainly meaning eye fatigue or eye strain. It is not yet a disease so much as a tired organ asking to be looked after.

the bright object is now a backlit display

why the classics call the eye a seat of fire

The granthas describe vision itself as a kind of fire. The function of seeing is carried by alochaka pitta, the form of pitta seated in the eye, and pitta is hot, sharp and light by nature. The eye is repeatedly named as a special seat of teja, the fire element (Sushruta Samhita, Uttara Tantra, the shalakya chapters on the eye). This is the old way of saying what we feel as heat and burning when the eyes are overworked.

Because the eye runs on fire, the classics warn that it is most easily harmed by more heat and more strain. Sushruta lists straining the sight, staring at bright or fine objects, sun and fire, sleeplessness, grief and suppressed tears among the causes of eye disorders (Sushruta Samhita, Uttara Tantra). Long hours at a bright screen, with too little sleep and too few blinks, sit squarely in that list, only the bright object is now a backlit display.

Ashtanga Hridaya, in its section on the eyes, frames good eye care around the opposite qualities: coolness, lubrication, rest and gentle nourishment (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana). The whole tradition leans the eye away from dry heat and toward cool and oily, which is why so much classical eye care involves ghee, cooling washes and shade.

gentle everyday care

The single most useful idea is rest from fixed focus. Both Sushruta and Ashtanga Hridaya count overstrained looking and looking at very bright things among the causes of eye trouble, so the cure begins with simply looking away (Sushruta Samhita, Uttara Tantra; Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana).

Cooling and lubrication are the other half. Triphala, the blend of amalaki, haritaki and bibhitaki, is the herb most tied to the eyes in the classics; Bhavaprakasha and Ashtanga Hridaya praise it for the sight, and a strained, filtered triphala water is a traditional eye wash (Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda; Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana). Ghee taken in food is the classic internal eye nourisher, again because it is cooling and lubricating where the eye tends to run dry and hot.

None of this is exotic. Blink more, look far away often, dim the dryness and heat around you, sleep enough, and feed the eyes a little ghee and triphala. It is the same advice the granthas gave to scribes and gem-cutters who strained their eyes long before screens existed.

when to see a doctor

This is general information from the classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. See an eye doctor or a qualified vaidya, and do not just self-treat, if you have eye pain that is severe or does not settle, sudden blurred or double vision, loss of vision or a dark curtain across your sight, flashes of light or a shower of new floaters, a red painful eye, sensitivity to light, discharge, or headaches with the eye trouble. Anyone with diabetes, glaucoma or recent eye surgery should be checked promptly rather than waiting. Always strain triphala water very finely and use clean water before bringing anything near the eye, and stop at once if it stings or worsens.

Try This Today

  • look far, often: Every 20 to 30 minutes, lift your eyes off the screen and gaze at something far across the room or out a window for half a minute. This is rest from the fixed near-focus the granthas warn against.
  • palming: Rub your palms together until warm, cup them gently over closed eyes without pressing, and rest in the dark warmth for a few slow breaths. A simple way to give strained eyes shade and stillness.
  • blink and moisten: We forget to blink at screens. Blink fully and slowly a few times each break to spread your own tears, the eye's natural cooling lubrication.
  • triphala eye splash: Soak a teaspoon of triphala powder overnight in a cup of clean water, strain it very finely through fine cloth twice, and use the clear water to splash or wash the closed eyes in the morning. Discard any leftover, never store it.
  • a little ghee in food: Add a small spoon of cow's ghee to your daily meal. The classics treat ghee as the internal nourisher of sight because it is cooling and lubricating where strained eyes run hot and dry.
  • protect your sleep: Sleeplessness sits on Sushruta's list of causes of eye trouble. Aim for unbroken night sleep and keep the last screen hour dim, so the eyes get their nightly rest.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Hours of screens leave your eyes dry, hot and aching. Ayurveda treats the eye as a seat of fire, and its old advice on rest, cooling and triphala still fits a workday glued to a glowing rectangle.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Ayurveda calls the eye a seat of fire, so screen strain shows up as heat, dryness and aching.
The classics blame overstrained looking, bright objects, dryness and lost sleep, which is modern screen life.
Care leans cool and oily: rest from fixed focus, more blinking, ghee in food, triphala eye care.

glossary_terms.definition: the form of pitta seated in the eye that carries vision
the fire element, which the classics seat strongly in the eye
the three-fruit blend of amalaki, haritaki and bibhitaki, classically tied to the eyes
the branch of Ayurveda dealing with diseases above the neck, including the eyes

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Uttara Tantra (shalakya, chapters on the eye)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana (netra care and eye disorders)
Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda (triphala and eye care)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "netra" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, netra is defined as: the eye

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to look far, often. Every 20 to 30 minutes, lift your eyes off the screen and gaze at something far across the room or out a window for half a minute. This is rest from the fixed near-focus the granthas warn against.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to palming. Rub your palms together until warm, cup them gently over closed eyes without pressing, and rest in the dark warmth for a few slow breaths. A simple way to give strained eyes shade and stillness.
